text: Recyclability and Enhanced Stability of Perovskite Solar Cells via Light Optimization Alkyl ammonium metal halides-based solar cells have drawn increasing attention owing to their high power conversion efficiency (PCE) and low preparation complexity, which suggest that perovskite solar cells are a promising candidate for commercialization as low-cost thin-film photovoltaics (PVs). Unfortunately, the stability of most perovskite solar cells under sunlight illumination remains a significant challenge, and it remains unclear how to effectively enhance their stability. Herein, we discover that the stability degradation under illumination is quasi-reversible and is highly dependent on the operating conditions of solar cells. Using various measurements including electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) on the methylammonium (MA) based and formamidinium (FA) based lead triiodide perovskite solar cells, as well as on the mixed-cation $MA_xFA_{1-x}$, we investigated the dynamic behavior of these devices and uncovered their underlying physical mechanisms. Variable-temperature EIS experiments were conducted on the $MA_xFA_{1-x}$ PSCs, from which the diffusion coefficients of the MA and FA ions were obtained. Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) experiments showed that the $MAPbI_3$ undergoes structural phase transition and exhibits lattice expansion at the standard operating temperature. Thus, based on our experimental results, the rapid stability degradation of $MAPbI_3$ under the illumination is ascribed to the thermal activation of the ions movement and the lattice expanding phase transition triggered by the infrared (IR) part of the solar spectrum. The stability of solar cells can be highly improved using a white-light LEDs, an infrared-cutoff filter, or even a cooling system.
